name: Volos
description: Volos, also known as Veles, is a major god in Slavic mythology, often associated with cattle, wealth, magic, trickery, and the underworld. His origins are somewhat obscured, but he's believed to be an ancient deity, possibly predating Perun, the god of thunder and lightning. Volos stands in opposition to Perun in many myths, representing the earthy, chthonic forces as opposed to Perun's celestial, ordered dominion. He is a protector of flocks and shepherds, a bestower of wealth, and a patron of musicians, poets, and storytellers. His association with magic and the underworld gives him a role as a guide of souls and a keeper of secrets. Volos is often depicted as a shapeshifter, able to take on animal forms, particularly that of a serpent, dragon, or bear. He is a complex figure, both revered and feared, embodying the wild, untamed aspects of nature and the mysteries of the unseen world. He is crucial to the prosperity of the land, ensuring the health of livestock and the abundance of harvests. Though often portrayed as an antagonist to Perun, their relationship is more of a dynamic balance, representing the duality of the cosmos and the cyclical nature of life and death. Volos also holds a place as a god of trade and merchants, further emphasizing his connection to worldly possessions and prosperity.
subtitle: God of cattle, wealth, and magic

keyMyths.title: The Theft of Cattle
keyMyths.description: Volos steals Perun's cattle, leading to a cosmic battle between the two gods. This conflict represents the struggle between chaos and order in the world.

keyMyths.title: Volos and the Underworld
keyMyths.description: Volos is the guardian of the underworld, guiding souls and holding the secrets of death. He is also connected to the earth's fertility and the cycle of rebirth.

keyMyths.title: Volos and the Veles Book
keyMyths.description: The Veles Book, a disputed historical text, portrays Volos as a protector of the Slavic people and a source of ancient wisdom. Its authenticity is debated among scholars.
